text.title: Decomposition Workflow
text.summary: Analyze-plan-execute-verify loop for splitting oversize files, with planner/executor subagent split and prompt patterns.

rule.statement: Stop the agent after the planning step and require explicit human approval before executing any moves; a wrong target tree cascades into many bad commits.
rule.rationale: Planning errors compound: 50 executor commits based on a flawed tree are far harder to revert than one rejected plan.

list.item: Analyze: identify file size (lines, tokens), list responsibilities, map dependencies, find natural boundaries.
Plan: draw target structure, define interfaces between new modules, order moves so tests stay green after each step.
Execute: create new files with empty structure, move code piece by piece, update imports, run tests after each move, commit each logical step.
Verify: all tests pass, no circular imports, each file has single responsibility, sizes within limits, naming is clear.

section.title: Subagent Architecture

list.item: Planner (Opus): reads oversize file + direct importers, emits target tree (file → responsibility → est. lines) and move-order respecting tests.
Executor (Sonnet): performs each Extract-Service / Extract-Component / Extract-Module step; runs test suite between commits; uses git mv.
Reviewer (Sonnet): checks SRP, file size, naming after each step; runs madge --circular / pydeps --show-cycles.

section.reference: Planner prompt: produce target tree + ordered move list as JSON.
Script to surface oversize files, high-complexity functions, and hotspots.

rule.statement: Pin a minimum file size of 30–50 LOC in every executor prompt to prevent micro-files.
rule.rationale: Agents over-decompose by default; the minimum enforces meaningful units.

rule.statement: Always pair a decomposition with a green test suite; if tests do not exist, write characterization tests before moving code.
rule.rationale: Without tests, any move can silently change behavior; characterization tests capture current behavior as a safety net.

rule.statement: Track churn x complexity (CodeScene-style); refactor only the top 5–10 hotspots first, not the entire repo.
rule.rationale: DORA research shows high-churn high-complexity files disproportionately drive incidents; targeted decomposition yields the highest ROI.
